page_title: Detective mode
text:
**Detective Mode** is a setting in Simple History that when enabled adds **detailed debug information** to each logged event.

The Detective mode is enabled under the general settings in *Settings » Simple History*.

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/detective-mode-settings-1024x206.png)Screenshot: Settings for Detective mode is available in Settings » Simple History » General.

Normally each logged event stores only the right amount of information needed to show a useful message for that specific event. This changes when Detective mode is enabled: each event will now store way more information. This information includes:

- PHP file executed, i.e. 
```
my-file.php
```
- Request URI used
- Debug backtrace summary
- User browser agent
- Current 
```
$_GET
```

 and
```
$_POST
```

page_title: Detective mode
text:
.php
```
- Request URI used
- Debug backtrace summary
- User browser agent
- Current 
```
$_GET
```

 and
```
$_POST
```

 payload
- An array with all [WordPress filters](https://developer.wordpress.org/plugins/hooks/filters/) fired until the event was logged
- The name of any cron job running
- Result from [php_sapi_name()](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.php-sapi-name.php), i.e. information about where request was made from: the cli or the web. The result can be many vales, including “apache2handler”, “fpm-fcgi”, “cli-server”, “litespeed”, “cli”.
- WP-CLI command/parameters used

All this information makes it more easy for a developer or security expert to track down exactly where, how, and why something was logged.

Please note that enabling Detective Mode means that all $_GET and $_POST data is logged and that this data can contain sensitive information. The plugin tries to remove common password related data but with the wide range of different plugins and code implementations we can not make sure all is removed.

page_title: Detective mode
text:
### Example

Here is a simple example that shows how this information can be useful. Below is a simple message saying that the translations for WooCommerce was updated. A curious person may start to wonder: but how and why was is updated by WP-CLI? With Detective mode enabled a lot of more information that this message has been stored. Let’s klick the date and time of the event to bring up the *context data table view*.

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/wp-cli-updated-translations-for-woocommerce.png)

Here in the context data table we have access to *a lot of more information*.

We can now see that WP-CLI was used to run WP-cron from the command line using this command
```
wp cron event run --due-now
```

and in the backtrace info we can follow the progress in details and see all function called, from initiating WP-CLI to running command on the 
```
Language_Pack_Upgrader
```

 class.

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/wp-cli-updated-translations-for-woocommerce-context-with-detective-data-1-545x1024.png)

page_title: Does it work with SQLite? (Yes it does!)
category: Does It Work With Sqlite Yes It Does
heading_path: Does it work with SQLite? (Yes it does!)
text:
Simple History does work with the [SQLite Database Integration](https://wordpress.org/plugins/sqlite-database-integration/) plugin from the WordPress team, meaning that you can use Simple History with [SQLite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQLite).

It’s a pretty cool way to run WordPress to be honest. No need to set up a MySQL or MariaDB database.

> SQLite Database Integration

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
heading_path: Is the Simple History plugin GDPR compliant?
text:
## Is the Simple History plugin GDPR compliant?

Maybe. It depends.

[No WordPress plugin in itself can provide legal compliance](https://make.wordpress.org/plugins/2018/04/12/legal-compliance-added-to-guidelines/), and the responsibility for making your website compliant with legislation lies with the site administrators.

The [WordPress Compliance Disclaimers FAQ says](https://developer.wordpress.org/plugins/wordpress-org/compliance-disclaimers/):

> Still, at the end of the day, the responsibility remains with the site administrators to ensure their sites meet the qualifications for any compliance.

So it depends on how you use the plugin. The [GDPR law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation) is complicated and we are not lawyers, so we can’t say if it’s compliant for your specific site or use case.

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
## What is GDPR?

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a comprehensive privacy law enacted by the European Union that gives individuals greater control over their personal data. It applies to all organizations that process the personal data of EU residents, regardless of where the organization is located.

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
heading_path: How Simple History Handles Your Data
text:
## How Simple History Handles Your Data

Simple History is a WordPress plugin that logs user activity on your website. Here’s what you need to know about how we handle data in relation to GDPR:

Some key GDPR-related points about Simple History:

- The plugin does not use [Google Fonts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Fonts)
- The plugin sets no [cookies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_cookie)
- The plugin uses no [local storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_storage)
- The plugin can store anonymized [IP addresses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IP_address)

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### What Data is Stored

Simple History logs and stores the following types of information:

- User actions (logins, post edits, setting changes, etc.)
- Timestamps of actions
- User IDs and usernames
- User emails
- IP addresses (can be anonymized – see below)
- Browser and device information

It’s important to note that the plugin logs information that can be considered “personal data” under GDPR, meaning information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. This includes usernames and email addresses in addition to other logged actions.

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### Where the Data is Stored

All logged information is stored directly in your WordPress database, specifically in these custom tables:

- ```
{$wpdb->prefix}simple_history
```

 – Stores the main log entries
- ```
{$wpdb->prefix}simple_history_contexts
```

 – Stores additional context information

This means:

- The data never leaves your server
- No data is sent to external services
- You maintain complete control over the stored information

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### Data Retention

By default, Simple History keeps log entries for 60 days, after which they are automatically deleted. You can [adjust this retention period](https://simple-history.com/support/change-number-of-days-to-keep-log/) to set it to any number of days.

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### IP Address Anonymization

To enhance privacy compliance, Simple History offers IP address anonymization:

1. Go to Settings → Simple History
2. Select “IP Address Anonymization”
3. Choose between:
- No anonymization (stores full IP addresses)
- Partial anonymization (e.g., 192.168.xxx.xxx)
- Full anonymization (no IP addresses stored, *coming soon*)

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text: ## GDPR Compliance Considerations

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### Data Controller Responsibilities

As the website owner using Simple History, you are considered the “data controller” under GDPR. This means:

- You determine the purposes and means of processing the personal data
- You are responsible for implementing appropriate security measures
- You must respond to data subject requests (access, deletion, etc.)

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
### Recommendations for GDPR Compliance

1. **Update your Privacy Policy** to mention:
- That you use Simple History to log user activities
- What data is collected and why
- How long data is retained
- How users can request access to or deletion of their data
2. **Configure data retention** to keep logs only as long as necessary
3. **Consider anonymizing IP addresses** to minimize personal data collection
4. **Implement a process** for handling data subject requests:
- Right to access their data
- Right to be forgotten (deletion)
- Right to data portability

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
## FAQs

**Q: Does Simple History set cookies?**
A: No, Simple History does not set or use cookies for tracking purposes.

**Q: Is Simple History compliant with GDPR?**
A: Simple History provides tools that help you maintain GDPR compliance, but compliance ultimately depends on how you configure and use the plugin. The plugin itself processes data only on your server and provides options for data minimization and retention management.

**Q: How can I delete all data for a specific user?**
A: You can search for all entries related to a specific user in the Simple History log interface and delete them manually. Alternatively, when you delete a user through WordPress, their username will remain in the logs but will no longer be linked to an active user account.

**Q: Do I need user consent to use Simple History?**
A: Since Simple History is used for legitimate administrative purposes and security monitoring, you may be able to process this data under the “legitimate interest” basis rather than consent. However, legal requirements vary, so consult with a legal professional regarding your specific situation.

page_title: GDPR and Privacy: How Your Data is Stored in Simple History
text:
## Need More Help?

If you have additional questions about Simple History and GDPR compliance, please [contact us](https://simple-history.com/contact/) or visit our [documentation](https://simple-history.com/documentation/).

Check out these related questions in the support forum:

- [GDPR Jetpack Google Fonts API](https://wordpress.org/support/topic/gdpr-jetpack-google-fonts-api/)
- [GDPR Compliance #250](https://wordpress.org/support/topic/gdpr-compliance-250/)
- [GDPR Compliance #236](https://wordpress.org/support/topic/gdpr-compliance-236/)
- [GDPR Compliance #66](https://wordpress.org/support/topic/gdpr-compliance-66/)
- [Is that plugin GDPR conform?](https://wordpress.org/support/topic/is-that-plugin-gdpr-conform/)

---

*This information is provided as general guidance and does not constitute legal advice. For specific legal questions regarding GDPR compliance, please consult with a qualified legal professional.*

page_title: Got a charge from “LEMSQZY* SIMPLE-HISTORY”?
category: Got A Charge From Lemsqzy Simple History
text:
Do you have a charge from Lemon Squeezy or LEMSQZY* SIMPLE-HISTORY and don’t know why?

It probably means that you or any other person with access to your card has bought any of [our add-ons](https://simple-history.com/add-ons/) .

Depending on your bank account and card issuer the charge can be shorter, for example: “LEMSQZY* SIMPLE-H” or “”LEMSQZY* SIMPLE-HI”.

You’ve probably noticed a charge from LEMSQZY* SIMPLE-HISTORY on your credit card or bank statement and not always understood why. We’ll clear up why you are seeing these charges and why it’s completely normal.

Related information:

- [https://www.lemonsqueezy.com/why-did-lemon-squeeezy-charge-me](https://www.lemonsqueezy.com/why-did-lemon-squeeezy-charge-me)
- [https://docs.lemonsqueezy.com/help/getting-started/statement-descriptor](https://docs.lemonsqueezy.com/help/getting-started/statement-descriptor)

page_title: How do I manage my add-on subscriptions
category: How Do I Manage My Add On Subscriptions
heading_path: How do I manage my add-on subscriptions
text:
You can [manage your subscription and billing settings](https://simple-history.lemonsqueezy.com/billing) using the [customer portal](https://simple-history.lemonsqueezy.com/billing).

On this customer portal you can:

- View payment history
- Download invoices
- Manage subscriptions and payment methods.

page_title: Troubleshooting errors when loading events
text:
Sometimes the log does not load correctly and it may be hard to understand why it’s not working.

You have much likely come here because you encountered a message like this while trying to load the log with events:

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/error-loading-events-details-1024x911.png)

There can be many different reasons for this error. It may be temporary or permanent. On this page we’ll try to give you some tips and help on how to fix this problem.

page_title: Troubleshooting errors when loading events
text:
### Common solutions

- Reload the page
- Check the server logs for errors
- Check the web browser console for errors
- Check the database server logs for errors

page_title: Troubleshooting errors when loading events
text:
### Common problems

Error 403: Cookie check failed (rest_cookie_invalid_nonce)
This can happen if you leave the history page open for a long time, without leaving it or reloading it. WordPress has a built in security check that causes the REST API calls to be invalid after a long period.

Solution: Reload/refresh the page using your browser reload-button and the problem should go away.

page_title: Troubleshooting errors when loading events
heading_path: Use the support forum for more solutions
text:
## Use the support forum for more solutions

Use the [support forum](https://wordpress.org/support/plugin/simple-history/) to search for similar issues or post a new question. The Simple History team – or other users with similar problems – will help you with your issue.

[Visit support forum](https://wordpress.org/support/plugin/simple-history/)

heading_path: Third party plugins with support for Simple History
text:
Plugins that have support for Simple History includes, but is not limited to:

- [Loginpetze](https://wordpress.org/plugins/loginpetze/)
- [Authorizer](https://wordpress.org/plugins/authorizer/)
- [Ad Inserter](https://wordpress.org/plugins/ad-inserter/)

Is this list missing a plugin? [Let us know!](https://simple-history.com/contact/)

page_title: Third party plugins with support for Simple History
category: Third Party Plugins With Support For Simple History
heading_path: Adding support for logging to Simple History in your plugin is easy
text:
## Adding support for logging to Simple History in your plugin is easy

If you are a plugin creator it’s very easy to add support in your plugin to log to Simple History. Check out the [Logging API](https://simple-history.com/docs/logging-api/) to get started. In just a few minutes you can get simple logging working!

> Logging API

page_title: Weekly email reports
text:
To modify the recipients of the Simple History weekly email reports go to Simple History » Settings and add or remove email addresses.

Click save when you’re done and the next monday the recipients will get a fresh summary of the last week!

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/email-report-email-settings-1024x483.png)

page_title: What is an initiator
text: Who is responsible for an event? Who made it happen? That’s what the initiator is for.

page_title: ⏰ Why is the time of my events wrong?
heading_path: ⏰ Why is the time of my events wrong?
text:
If the time shown in the log is some hours before or behind your computer time, please check your **timezone setting**:

- Go to WP Admin › Settings › General.
- Check if the “Local time is” is your current/wanted time. If not – set it to your timezone.
- Now events in the log should use correct times!

![](https://simple-history.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/image-1024x373.png)
